CURBRENT AFFAIRS,
The LEADER this week is enlarged to 28 pages.

The sentence of five, years penal servitude on the
two Englishwomen will not be considered too much by
the public opinion of Treland : many people ‘will con-
sider it too little. It is bad enough to have a.gang of
militant Suffers here—a - gang: who would never have
come into existence bub that from one molive or
another they were induced to imitate the English
movement—yithout having imported would-be theatre
burners. With the Suffragette movement in England
we have little concern, but when two Bnglishwomen
come over to Ireland to carry on their quarrel in a
mosb eriminal manner with the English Prime Minister,
who “happens to be in Treland on o Home Rule
mission, we think they richly deserve the sentences
$hat have been passed upon them.

‘The theory of rebellion preached by Bonar Law and
Carson is one of the most dangerous and ill-omened

forces in the British world to-day, and it has a direct
concern with us in Ireland also. The sympathy strike
is full of dangers if carried oub by ignorant and reck-
less men, but it is doubtful if it is nc' less ominous
than the mew doctrine about rebellion which has
alresdy largely helped to create the Belfast atrocities,
and which is—it is quite plain to see—corrupting and
misleading the minds of large sections of the public.
Bonar Law has broken out against Parliamentary
Government as the underworld in Dublin broke out last
August.  Bonar Law stands for at least partial
anarchy, and apart from present politics or a wish to
wmake a point against Unionists, every thinking: man
must be really alarmed at the probable consequences
on the public mind in these countries of the position
taken up “by Bonar Law. Let us again put. that
position in the words used by Asquith:— 38
The moment you lay down, as the leaders of the
stitutional party lay down, the doctrine that a
T do not care what minority—if you like,
- led, because a particular Act
eful to their views, and, as
ive to their interests, to resist
absolute end to Parliamentary

of leg

they think, oppre
it by force, there is an
government.

When the Leader of the Opposition in England—the
figurehead of the TUnionist Party—takes up that atbi-
tude it is nob so surprising that people who think they
have a public grievance go oub window breaking and
hatchet- throwing. It seems to us that the full imporb
of Bonar Law’s utterly lawless attitude is not grasped
by the more or less intelligent section of his followers
yeb.

We are not surprised that certain people are dragging
in incidents of moder Irish history to bolster up their
folly or criminality as the case may be. Wee Tim
tried to lay contribution on the Clerkenwell Explosion
in favour of his client, who was concerned in the
attempt to burn the Theatre Royal in Dublin.  What-
ever relevance such  references might have
in England, W here, for all we know to
the contrary, the Suffragette movement may,
be a national reality, they had nothing to do
iyith foreighers coming over here to burn a public
building in Ireland. In so far as the Skeffy gang are
concerned these arguments have no relevance, as the
movement here is what the Americans call a ‘“ fake "
—it is a pure exotic that has attracted a few ‘cranks
that might claim to be natives of the country. Aects
of historic Irish violence have no relevant bearing on
the Skeffy gang. - Ireland had admitted uational
grievances, and those who might not admit that there
were grievances had to admit that the people as &
whole laboured under the sense of grievance. The
Land Teague was a national affair, and not what could
be called a Davitt gang; @ 1d the Fenians were not an
oxotic coterie. No responsible person attemapted to
justify any outrages that mavked the Land Teague
struggle. In fact, during the Land Teague, only thab
the people were Catholies and were held in check by
the force of Catholic ethics, and that the
clergy restrained them, the outrages and shootings thab
accompanied that struggle would not have been the
mere breaking away of the wilder and less restrained
apirits: if the people were not Catholics, the landlords,
and the people who were part and parcel of the alien
landlord system, would probably have been shot at the
rate of a dozen a waelk duting the intense years of the
land war. The full force of the law was put against

.

those who, did break it during that fight;;and:the;

and the gallows consumed their prey. ~ Indi
outbreaks of crime were understood during that time, ;
but no responsible persons ever ‘attempted 'to justify
them; ‘and ‘those who committed them and were
caught—and many who were caught and did not commit
hem—vere made to suffer.

. There is no parallel in point of fact between the
‘ridiculous Skeffy: gang, composed partly of foreigners,
“h ; and, say; the Land League agitation. Again, the Land
_Wwar was really an international rather than a civil quar-
vel: and those who broke the law, whether they hap-
pened to be morally justified or were both morally and

" legally criminals, had to take their punishment, and

lcerbéminly it was measured out to them with no niggard
hand.

)

The exotic and ridiculous Suffer coterie in this
;- counfry can find no extenuation for criminality in any
crimes that may have punctuated the land war. But
'+ they and others may be able to catch on to, and use,
+ i the doctrine of rebellion preached by the head of the
Unionist Party in England. If a few people in Ulster
may take up arms against the King because they may
“not like one of his laws, and when the head of the
# Opposition, Mr. Bonar Law, stands over that disloyal,
unconstitutional and lawless proposition, one must
expect that it will create great confusion in many
. minds, and be used by unscrupulous people to attempt
‘to justify propositions intrinsically as unconstitutional
- ‘and lawless as the position with regard to Home Rule
‘that Bonar Law has taken up.

The antics of the member for one of the divisions of
' ““ Rotten ”’ Cork cease to be even amusing, and they
are becoming very nearly worthless as a matter for
*“copy ’’ for us. Tim the Tory ‘‘ Teller ”’ makes only
- a very second-rate understudy of Skeffy. After all,
Skeffy is a ‘‘ character,” and Tim once was a ‘‘ char-
acter ’’ in his own line, but as a sort of imitation
Skeffy he is not doing justice to himself. We do not
see much potential prospects before the part of ‘‘ Tim
the Suffer,”” even should he go the length of adopting
Imickerbockers like the ‘‘old firm.”” TImitations are
always secondary matters.

One of the curious things concerning the Suffragette

. * trials last week was that the woman who was de-
fended by Tim the Suffer was convicted, whilst the
woman who conducted her own defence scored a dis-
.agreement of the jury. We would be slow indeed to
deduce from these particular facts that a criminal is a

fool who employs a lawyer to defend him, but the

facts are rather nasty for the ‘‘ Rotten Cork "’ mem-

ber.: Tim seemed to be more concerned with pouring
_alleged ““scorn’’ at the Hibernians than af keeping to the

- * facts of the case. Wee Tim is soured beyond restraint
at the success of Wee Joe. If he had more sense and

«  more control of his petty temper he would not be so
- free with public indications of his jealousy of the mem-

- ber for West Belfast. As it is Wee Joe appears to act

THE BALLYPEWTER TESTIMONIAL

" Dear friends; an occasion’s at hand
In which people, decent and nobby, 54
In high-minded action may band 4
To give a big purse to a bobby. - A
The hat at the present is passed,
For our village mentor and tutor,
The great Sergeant Blinkers, at last
Ts going to leave Ballypewter

So kindly come out with the dross,

And make up a purse for the peeler.
Just think what a terrible loss

He'll be to each poor honest dealer.
The coast on a Sunday was clear,

No bona in vain was a suitor,
Tor all he could swallow in beer,

While Blinkers ruled o’er Ballypewter.

The way certain peelers do act,

Disturbing some traffic is awful,
But Blinkers had genius and tact

And knew when a trade was quite lawful.
Tor guiding and ruling a place

No man than the sergeant was cuter,
And that's why we send in this case

The hat for him round Ballypewter.

With confidence do I appeal
To noble-souled people and thinkers,
To solidly show how they feel
Regarding the merits of Blinkers.
-They’re such as have often been sung,
And found not a single disputer,
So send your subscriptions to Bung,
The Drunkery Bar, Ballypewter.
A M. W.

- the already discontented staff of the ** National ”” Bank
" Is seething with indignation. ~What has’ Sir Thomas
Esmonde, ex-Sinn Feiner, to say for himself? And
whet has that old word spinner and paper patriot, Mr.
. Barry O’Brien, to say for himself? If the shareholders
wish to protect their interests, they ought to look after
the treatment of the staff. Depreciation of Consols and
other gilt-edged securities, bad harvests, and other such
adverse conditions right themselves in time, but the
loss of confidence by a staff in its directorate is not a
temporary calamity. n

. One glering measure of the Anglicisation of Ireland
is ‘the obvious interest that people in this country take
in Englard, and the almost absolute lack of concern in
Lngland about Ireland. A friend of ours is, or was
recently, in a place called Gateshead in some part of
England, and in a letter to us he incidentally mentions :
“* T'inquired at all the big—some very big—newsegents’
places. I think out of twenty places, iccluding London
agents, Hinckley, Bedford, Crewe, Euston Station,
Cambridge, Manchester, Carlisle, Newcastle, Leicester,
ete., and in no place could I find a single Irish paper.
The “‘Irish Timse’’ was on sale in one place in London.
Fancy the inroads of the English journals into poor, old,
i silly Irelend, and not a trace of Irish journalism over
here. Tt is a queer state of things, but it is ‘absolutely
true, however distressing, to an Irishman.””

Sir R. Egerton, of the Post Office, has received a
good deal of notice from time to time from us. His
correspondence with us, when he attempted, unsuc-
cessfully, to compel us to pay extra postage on our
sccond last Christmas number, will be in the recol-
| 1 lection of our readers. But bad as he is, at his worst
his retention in office is preferable to the importation
of a Mr. Norway, an English Protestanb, who, accord-
ing to the ““ Irish Times,” is to succeed to the secre-
taryship of the Post Office in Ireland in a few weeks.
Of course the ‘‘ Irish Times '’ had no okjection to offer
in ite columns to this announced dump, and we are not
2 surprised at Murphy’s Ha’penny Dreadful keeping
| =% silent also. But what of the ‘‘ Freeman?”’ It was
equally dumb. We appear to have to swallow a lot
of bitter doses whilst our representatives are keeping
the Government in power. Surely if the Irish Party

i had the will they need only say the word. and a job

* like this would be dropped by the ‘‘ Likeral "’ Govern-

ment like a hot potato. Since the above was written

i the Dublin Corporation, we are glad to see, has ex-

pressed its view on the matber, and so in Tuesday's

issue there was a weak and belated leaderette in the
‘‘ Freeman ’’ in reference to it.

o We are told that we are on the eve of Home Rule. The
Post Office Department is to be transferred under the
Bill to the Irish Government. The Government's
case is that Ireland is at this moment ripe and ready
for the management of its own affairs. Yet Mr. Herbert

Samuel, one of the most active supporters of Home
Rule, will, it is said, send an English official to control
the Irish Post Office.  Will even he have the hardi-
hood to declare thet in the whole Irish service there is
not an Irish official fit for the post ?  If he does, where
are the administrative abilities of Irishmen, to which
the Prime Minister paid such glowing tribute recently
in Dublin? What has the Irish Party to say? and
what is it going to do?

Who, by the way, is Mr. Norway, and wﬁy is he
dumped over here? We have made enquiry into ths
extraordinary state of affairs, and we are told that Sir
Reginald Egerton is still some years under 65 years of
age, and that there is no necessity for the P.M.G. to
say good-bye unless Sir R. Egerton himself wishes to
go. Has he been asked to stay—or has his departure
Deen facilitated to make way for Mr. Norway ?  If Sir
R. Egerton’s retirement was foreseen (and we are told
its probability was a matter of open knowledge in the
Post Office Department in London and Dublin), did it
occur to Mr. Samuel to provide for an Irish successor?
If not, why not? About six weeks ago it was known,
]o)r T)cl{mted, that this Mr. Norway had taken a house in

ubln.

The Irish Post Office has for generations been ruled
from London, and from Dublin also, by unsympathetic
Englishmen, who have never gained the confidence of
the people or the respect of the staff. If that evil tra-
dition must survive, even under a Home Rule admini-
stration kept in power by Irish votes, one cannot be
surprised at sceptical Nationalists asking ‘" cui bono’’ ?
There is time even yet for the people toc make them-
selves heard. Let the representative bodies all speak
out, quickly and plainly.  Sir R. Egerton is due to
retire on 80th September next. = Why should he go
that Mr. Norway may be accommodated > Mz. Samuel
will have to tell us; and if there is not in all the Irish
P.0. a man fit to be secretary, some of the existing
chief Trish officials are evidently unfit for their posts.
‘Who is to blame for this alleged poverty of ability and
intelligence ? Any ordinary business man can readily see
that, if such ignorance, widespread and undoubted,
exists, the heads of {he department in London are to
blame for permitting it to grow. But we in Ireland
know, and they in London know, that the whole game
that is being played is dishonest and unscrupulous.
It is a slander to say that Mr. Norway, whoever he
may be, who for years, if he comes over here, will be
acquiring knowledge of Ireland from his subordinates,
is better equipped intellectually or technically for: the
administration of the Irish P.O. than all the existing
Trish officials. This matter will have to be probed to
the bottom, and we shall return to the subject. Mean-
while let us again advise our public bodies to speak out,
and let individual Irishmen also publish theit resent-
ment at this latest and grossest insult offered to Irish-
men.

ously held' by a Protestant, or, to be more accurate,

 known. as the ‘White Mice.” What their religious

on such occasions, there was a full muster of magis-
 trates present—a thing' which probably has not

is. the only Catholic magistrate in_an overwhelmingly

position—two Catholics and one Protestant. ~One of
the Catholic candidates was a Head-constable of the
~ R.I.C., who is about to retire from the force, and who
had the permission of the Inspector General of the
R.IC. to apply for the vacant Clerkship. ~The other
Catholic candidate is a trader in Kilecullen. He knew
he had no chance of getting the position. He went for-
ward-:to test the tolerance of the majority of the magis-
trates. The only Protestant candidate is a sort of
gentleman farmer, residing in the district. He is a
returned South African, and has recently identified him-
- self very prominently with the anti-Home Rule move-
ment in Co. Kildare, and is an avowed admirer of
Carson, Craig, and Co. The applications of the
several candidates having been read, they wera told
t6 retire while the magistrates were voting.  After a
prolonged delay the candidates were called to hear the
result. The result of the voting was that the only
Protestant candidate was elected by a majority of four
to one. Major Thackery, R.M., supported the Head-
constable. Mr. Doyle voted for the Catholic trader
of Kilcullen. The other four—all Protestants and
bigots—voted for the only Protestant candidate. And
this notwithstanding that one of the Catholic candi-
dates, when canvassing the magistrates before the
election, was assured by more than one of the Pro-
testant magistrates that what they wanted was a man
who could keep the magistrates straight in conducting
the business of the court. If that were really the sort
of man they wanted, the Head-constable was the can-
_didate . to elect, who, after his long service in the
R.I.C.,, and having attained the rank of Head-
constable, should certainly be better qualified to keep
their‘ worships straight in conducting the business
of the court than the candidate that had been elected,
who probably has not been much in a Petty Sessions

The bluff and bluster about the ‘‘ladies’ who
commit crimes and call themselves Suffragettes is
becoming nauseating. We in Ireland have too much
reason to fear that Unionist money and Unionist tactics
are prominently behind this campaign.  We see by
a newspaper item from London that some people who
call themselves the ‘‘ Women’s Social and Political
Union "’ regard the sentence of five years’ penal servi-
tude on the women, Leigh and Evans, as ‘‘ an outrage.”
We are more concerned with the ‘blackguard outrage
of sending over women from Ingland to fire a theatre
in Dublin. The so-called Irish Suffers are not only so
unsexed, but so denationalised that they are taking the
part of these English criminal invaders, if we may take
it “that their freak newspaper organ represents their
views. It appears that some of the Skeffy gang in
prison are to refuse to enjoy certain privileges unless
““ the political status of their fellow-Suffragists from
England is duly recognised.”” A lot the Irish public
care what the Skeffy gang does.

There is a lot of cry-baby about this Suffer business,
and it is utter folly and fallacy to attempt to make out
in cold blood that people who attempt to fire a theatre
because they want a change in the laws are not crazy
criminals but high-minded politicians. There has been a

good deal of law-breaking in Irish political agitation.

But for one thing it was an international agitation.
Crime was always condemned, and no responsible
people ever attempted to justify things that would cor-
vespond to hatchet throwing, theatre burning, or whole-
sale window smashing. But crime often occured,
and it must ab least be said that there was no cry-baby
about the men who did the. crimes when they were
caught. They took their punishment at least like men.,
1t is a new thing, and a thing that cannot be tolerated,
that persons seeking a reform in the law—a reform
that in Ireland at least is not backed- up by popular
opinion, but is really only a trumped-up claim of a
coterie—should sit down and say, ‘“ We are politicians;
we will break windows, and when we are caught will
ask to be taken to prison in a motor car; we will petition
the Lord Lieutenant; we will not eat if our English
sisters who tried to burn a theatre are not treated to
luxuries as we are.”” This cry-baby business is being
carried too far, and the public are sick of it and of the
Skeffy gang. At the back of it all we can see the
Unionist gloating; and we suspect that Unionists’
money is largely financing the enterprise in England,
and the Skeffy gang are only a tail to the Tnglish
Suffers.

. Last. month we drew attention to a case in connec-
tion with the Meath branch of Lady Aberdeen’s
Women’s National Health Association. — The job has
now been done.  On the unanimous recommendation
of t,h.eir medical sub-committee, the W.N.H.A. have
appointed a Mr. Fred. W. Warren, of Navan, to be
Medical Superintendent of the Tuberculosis Dispen-
saries in Co. Meath. ~ What has Meath to say to the
giving away of £500 a year of public money to this
gentleman?  What are Mr. Warren’s qualifications ?
We learn that he had been a chemist in Navan for
several years, and as recently as last year qualified at
the examination of the Royal College of Surgeons,
Dublin, so that practically with very little medical
experience he has been pitchforked into this lucrative
position in Meath, which it would seem to us ought to
require special skill and training. Practitioners of

- extended experience have been passed over. Of course
Mr. Warren is one of the ‘‘ saved.”” The most lament-
able fact in connection with the matter is that the
County Council of Meath handed over the whole work-
ing of this Tuberculosis scheme to the Women’s

‘“saved ”’ goes on whilst the ‘“idolaters” are over-

years ago. ‘Even if it had been a Fenian murder, we:

~ ANOTHER ANNIVERSARY.

AST week we completed our tweltth year, and

to-day we begin owr thirteenth, and we do not
hink that any of our readers could say that he detected
- any sign or signs of our getting tired. ~ This week, owing
to the rush of advertisements, we had to enlarge the
paper to 28 pages, and it is a good thing to commence,

. amew year in that way.

: The LEADER, a fighting Irish paper, independent of

.all parties and organisations, was perhaps never more

wanted than at the present time. We are ready for
the situation if we get Home Rule, and we are ready for
it if, unfortunately, the cup should be dashed from Ire-
land’s lips again.  The worst of waiting for Home Rule
is, that the waiting period supplies a great excuse for
many people for marking time, and Ireland cannob
afford to mark time in any phase of its national activity.
Thé tendency not to embamrass the Government is
another drawback to the waiting period. We all under-
stand that when a Government is commiftted to pass
Home Rule in its lifetime, that it has a special claim
on Ireland, but we fear that many tricks and jobs are
played off on us in the meantime, whilst the pressure on
the Government, is not kept up to its proper strength.
The trick that the Government have decided on in
dumping an Englishman over here as Secretary of the
Post Office is a case in point as to what can be done to

‘ our detriment whilst we are waiting for Home Rule.
* This Post Office case, however, is too flagrant, and the

country should rise up against it and say: ** We will
not have Norway.”” Indeed, the country ought to keep
a special look out for big and little tricks that may be
attempted in Ireland at this critical period. We will
have to take over Ireland as a going concern and work
it, and we have enough rubbish, importations and gar-
rison jobocrats to carry without our quietly putting up
with this attempt to dump a Post Office secretary over
here.

An “anniversary number suggests a review of the
situation, yeb the fact that Home Rule is on the front
of the programme rather hinders one; you do not feel
equal to speculating on the situation on the assump-
tion that we will get Home Rule, whilst the prospects
are so hopeful one has not energy to speculate on the
basis that Home Rule is not coming in the very near
future. And the future of Ireland will be so funda-
mentally affected by the passing of Home Rule, or by
the failure to pass it, that even the mind is for the time
being placed in the mood of *“ wait and see.”” Irish
Treland and all it stands for cannot rest even in this
uncertain period, and our readers, each in his or her
sphere, has plenty of work to do.

The great strength of the LEADER, after its twelve
years’ work, is one of the healthiest and most encourag-
ing signs in Ireland.

- have continued to give the LEADER readers some

last week was on the right lines. We want the facts of

men who.are sent here from other parts of the country,

things even in normal times. They cannot understand:
it, and they spend a considerable time here before they,
begin to realise how things really are. I am strongly
of opinion that the various public bodies throughout the
country, who are expressing sympathy with us now,
imagine that Belfagt is in a very abnormal sfate at
present, and that with the exception of these periodic
outbursts, life is tolerable enough for Catholics in this
city. No doubt these occasions are exceptions, in the
sense that ascendancy bigotry does not always go the
length of murder, and that a standing army in the
public streets is not always necessary for the protection
of human life in broad daylight. But, and I wish your
readers to talke note of this, for I speak from knowledge,
with few exceptions no factory, mill or busizess house
in Belfast will employ a Catholic if they can help it.
This is true not of the Twelfth season merely;
it is true from January to December. Almost
every week we hear of Catholics being dismissed
for ~no other reason than the mere fact
that they are Catholics and have been found
out to be such. There is next to no employment for
Catholics in Belfast’s big business houses, which are
supported mainly by the intolerant, persecuting
Papishes of the terrible South and West. And the
irony of the situation is this, that Belfast trade would
come to a standstill in one week were it not for the
Catholic money from the rest of Ireland circulating in
the banks. Belfast is as poor as Lazarus, notwith-
standing zall their windy boasting about progress and
prosperity, wealth and intelligence. ~ The prosperity
and wealth are about equal to the intelligence, and ail
combined have about as much foundation in fact as the
civil and religious liberty. Yet the industrial and com-
mercial barbarians can bluff and bully and boss Catholic
Ireland, and gulling the Catholic traders of the South
and West, who never knew what persecution or intoler-
ance was, except to bear it, they can continue to send
out their wares to Catholic Ireland and refuse a chance
of livelihood to Catholics at home. They can draw on
Catholic money in the banks, and with such a swagger
that one would imagine they had a right to it; and
really the old spirit of ascendancy and superiority makes
them believe they have a right to it. We are appealing
for funds to relieve distress amongst the victims of
blinded, bigoted, devilish intolerance. We appeal to
Catholic Treland, and,with some few exceptions, we can
only appeal to Catholics. The Catholics are coming
to our aid. -But Catholic Ireland can come far more
effectively to our aid than by sending subscriptions to
a relief fund. The case of the Catholics in Belfast is
and has been desperate, and desperate diseases demand
drastic remedies.  If you hit Belfast’s pocket, it will
not sband the religious test long. ;

THE LEADER.

have bsen cruel and selfish spendthrifts and tyrants, nio
student of Irish History can for a moment doubt. But

not all. ' There have been some notable and noble’ ex-.

geptgons—men with humane hearts, who felt for the

inevitable poverty of their tenantry, and who by their

consideration, or even generosity, won for themselves
the blessings and the prayers of the suffering poor.

But besides these, there have been many landlords held

in odium by their tenantry and whose names have been

handed on to posterity, branded with the stigma of
cruelty or injustice, who did not deserve the evil reputa-
tion they received. And why? Because of the evil
and blinding influence exercised upon them by that

“miserable class of human beings known as land agents.

If the landlord has been one of Ireland’s scourges, o

too has been the land agent; and of the two the land

agent has been the more persistent and the more con-
temptible.

Amongst the land agent class thefe have also been
some splendid exceptions—practical, humane men wha
understood and relieved the poverty of the tenants.
But, as a class, the land agent has been the bitter,
relentless, unfeeling enemy of the people.  And the
reasons are not far to seek. As a rule the land agent
class has been recruited from the ranks of the broken-
down gentry, or retired army officials, or needy solici-
tors—men almost invariably opposed to the national,
political and religious ideals of the people.. Many
of these men, smarting under the stigma of poverty
brought upon them by their own extravagance or de-
bauchery, sought, as it were, to revenge themselves
upon the innocent tenantry whom they despised or
hated, and amongst whom they could not tolerate the
appearance of contentment and prosperity. They re-
garded the people, more or less, as serfs or slaves, to be
oppressed and plundered as their miserable jealousy or
hatred suggested. 5

It seems like stating a truism to say that land agents
have been one of the greatest causes of Ireland’s woes.
They were mere stereotyped, rent-collecting machines,
without heart or feeling, and with, as a rule, very little
brains or intelligence.. They received a percentage on
rents collected, and hence, being half beggars them-
selyes, and having no sympathy with the ideals or
aspirations of the people, they acted without either
consideration or justice. If the agents had been humane,
or actuated by Christian principles, they would have
regarded the interests and the betterment of the tenan-
try as their special care.  Yet, not one in a hundred
seemed to care as much about the welfare of the
tenants as they did about the dog that followed at their
heels. Contempt and vindictiveness seemed to devour
their narrow, miserable hearts.

Many a landlord has been cursed or blamed unfairly,
and has earned the reputation of being a tyrant, owing
to the trickery and injustice of his agent. The agent
Lept matters in his own hands, and kept from the know-
ledge of, his employer circumstances concerning the
tenantry, which, if known, would have saved much
suffering and great injustice. For it must be acknow-
ledged, that many a landlord would never have acted as
he did, if the bare, naked truth concerning his tenantry,
had' been told him by his agents. It is beyond doubt
or question, the right of any tenant tc approach his
landlord and explain the circumstances of his case.
And yet, for doing so, tenants, as a rule, had to suffer

the vengeance and vindictiveness of the agent. Agents
seemed to usurp to themselves the right of acting as
judge, jury, witness and all in cases of dispute between
themselves and the tenantry; and they regarded it as
presumption and insolence that a tenant should give
his version of his circumstances to the landlord. And
this same agent was merely the hired employce of the
landlord,, though he generally regarded himself as a
Iind of petty local autocrat, whose duty it was to bully,
terrerize and plunder the impoverished tenants.

. Many a poor farmer, struggling hard to rear his
family in stinted decency, has had his farm talken from
him, through the vindictiveness or greed of the agent,
that the agent himself, or some friend of his, might step
in and grab it. © Many a’respectable farmer, who by

‘Telephone 1294,

Avcust 17, 1912.

hard labour and good business capacity, was raising
himself in the world, has had his hopes blighted and
the prospects of himself and his family ruined by the
petty jealousy or religious spite of a narrow-minded
land agent. How often has a poor widow and her help-
less children been driven to the workhouse or left stary-
ing on the roadside by the grinding, un-Christian cruelty
of the agent? One of the many mean, despicable
devices employed by some agents to actually rob the
tenantry was the refusal of receipts. The tenant,
especially if a poor widow, was told, after paying the
rent, that the agent was too busy and that the receipt
would be sent by post. That receipt seldom, if ever,
came; and later on the tenant was forced to pay that
rent over again, the land agent having pocketed the
money. I have known such cases to repeatedly occur;
and, let me add, that one of these agents has since
fallen from a state of luxury and prosperity back into
that poverty to which he drove so many.

Another common means of oppression and injustice
was the raising of the rent; and for this the agent was
almost invariably to blame.  An industrious or im-
proving tenant who endeavoured to brighten his home-
stead, or who by dint of constant toil had much im-
proved the quality of his holding, was at once pounced
upon by the vigilant, vindictive agent, his rent was
raised and thereby his hope of profit was destroyed and
his prospects of betterment blighted .

Thus it is that in the past one of the most persistent
and prolific causes of Irish emigration has been the
Trish land agent. His petty vindictiveness and jeal-
ousies, his narrow-minded bigotry and his blind greed
and injustice have ruined many a prospect of success,
have blighted and emptied many a home, filled many
an emigrant ship, and scattered the Trish peasantry over
the face of the earth.

As the scourge of landlordism slowly passes from the
land, so with it passes the second, and perhaps the
more bitter scourge—the land agent. He has been a
blister and a fruitful source of evil in our land for cen-
turies; and now that he is going or gone, let us hope
and pray that Ireland may not look upon his like for
many a century yet to come.

The recent bitter anti-Catholic and anti-clerical
agitation, in which such contemptible specimens of
Christianity as ‘ Mike the Catholic '’ and ““ Pat,”” ete.,
were engaged, was largely propagated and fostered by
the land agent class. That agitation aimed at be-
littling the religion of the people and the people them-
selves, and particularly at belittling and hounding down
their priesthood. And why? Because their priests
had stood by and with the people against the inhuman
treatment and heartless exactions of both landlord and
agent in the past; and because now they were coun-
selling and advising their people against unjust and
extortionate bargains for their holdings, which the
greed of heartless agents strove to impose upon them.
And for that crime—the crime of defending a helpless
or defenceless people, their clergy were systematically
belittled and belied, and made the target of every vile
slander and cowardly insinuation that could be raked up
from the antiquated dust-bins of ancient and modern
bigotry.

If you want muddled reasoning, we suppose a chair-
man of the Bass ale business is an appropriate place
to look for it. At the recent meeting the Bass chair-
man said that the Government’s proportion of their
earnings was 75 per cent. and the shareholders 25 per
cent.! Did reason ever stagger so at a public meeting?
Of course the money that is paid in licenses and taxes by,
Bass or other Bungs is part of the cost of production
and is not paid out of profits. If there were no taxes
the Bass people would not find their customers so
befuddled as to pay the same price as they now pay
for ale and taxes.  The customers now buy ale and
taxes; were there no taxes they would only buy ale,
and the Bass people would have to reduce their prices
and correspondingly decrease their revenue.
